content.text: Platform adoption and growth metrics measure whether the AI developer platform is genuinely spreading through the engineering organization and delivering compounding value — or whether it is stagnating at the same early-adopter teams while the majority of engineers build shadow solutions outside its scope. Without these metrics, platform investment decisions are made on anecdote rather than evidence.
What it is

Platform adoption metrics are a funnel of behavioral signals that track the developer journey from first awareness of the platform through to active usage and team-level advocacy. The funnel mirrors standard product growth frameworks (activation, retention, referral) adapted for an internal platform audience. Growth metrics add time-series dimension: are these funnel stages improving, stagnating, or regressing quarter-over-quarter?

The core adoption metrics are: Monthly Active Users (MAU, the number of distinct developers with at least one platform interaction in the last 30 days), Team Penetration Rate (the percentage of AI-eligible engineering teams with at least one active developer), Net Promoter Score, Shadow Infrastructure Rate (the percentage of LLM API calls in the organization that bypass the platform), and new-team activation rate (teams onboarded this quarter vs. target).
Why it matters

A platform that a few enthusiastic early-adopter teams love but that 80% of the organization ignores is not delivering organizational value proportional to its investment. Platform teams often mistake internal NPS from existing users for adoption: a platform used by 3 teams with NPS +40 is not the same as a platform used by 20 teams with NPS +15. Both numbers matter independently.

The shadow infrastructure rate is often the most actionable adoption metric: a high shadow rate (developers calling LLM APIs directly, maintaining their own rate limiters, building their own cost tracking) indicates that the platform is not meeting enough needs to be worth adopting. Measuring shadow infra reveals the true competitive landscape the platform faces — not external competitors, but internal workarounds.
Benefits

Investment justification: growth metrics translate platform adoption into business terms that engineering leadership understands — X% reduction in shadow infrastructure, Y new teams shipping AI features faster.
Targeting improvement: funnel drop-off analysis shows exactly where in the adoption journey developers are being lost, enabling targeted fixes.
Shadow infra reduction: measuring and publicizing the shadow infrastructure rate creates organizational pressure to consolidate on the platform.
Retention early warning: a drop in 90-day retention among previously active developers signals regressions before they show up in NPS.
Competitive positioning: comparing MAU growth against the number of new AI projects starting in the organization reveals whether the platform is winning or losing to shadow solutions.
Resource planning: MAU and team count trends inform headcount planning — each new tier-1 consumer increases on-call load and support volume.
Trade-offs and limitations
Definition sensitivity: MAU can be defined as any API call, or only as a successful deploy — the denominator matters as much as the count.
Eligibility estimation: the denominator for Team Penetration Rate (eligible teams) requires knowing which teams are doing AI work, which itself requires periodic surveying.
Shadow infra undercounting: direct vendor API calls from individual developer laptops may not be detectable through network monitoring; shadow infrastructure is always an undercount.
Lagging vs. leading indicators: MAU is a lagging indicator of platform quality changes made months ago. Use TTFAD and friction score as leading indicators that predict future MAU trends.
Growth pressure misalignment: optimizing for MAU growth can lead to aggressive onboarding of teams that are not ready, inflating short-term numbers while increasing support load and churn.
How it works

The platform API gateway logs every request with developer_id and team_id. A daily aggregation job computes MAU (unique developer_ids with at least one request in the last 30 days), active teams (unique team_ids with at least one request), and total token volume. The Shadow Infrastructure Rate is computed by cross-referencing platform usage logs with cloud billing data: if the organization's total LLM API spend is $100K and the platform intermediated $60K of that, the shadow rate is 40%.

The activation funnel is computed from the TTFAD journey event stream: activation rate is the percentage of developers who emit a T0 (platform access granted) event and subsequently emit a T4 (first production deploy) event within 30 days. The 90-day retention rate is the percentage of developers who were active in a given month and are still active 90 days later.
NPS measurement

Platform NPS is measured monthly via a 3-question Slack pulse sent to all developers with at least one platform interaction in the last 30 days. Question 1: NPS score (0-10). Question 2 for Detractors (0-6): 'What is the single biggest reason for your score?' Question 3 for Promoters (9-10): 'What is the single thing that makes the platform valuable?' Responses feed into the friction register and are summarized in the monthly engineering digest.
Leading vs. lagging indicators

MAU and NPS are lagging indicators: they reflect decisions developers made weeks or months ago about whether to adopt or stay on the platform. TTFAD and friction score are leading indicators: they predict future MAU trends. A platform team that sees its friction score rising should expect MAU to stagnate or decline in 6-8 weeks unless corrective action is taken. Managing adoption requires watching both: lagging indicators to understand where you are, leading indicators to understand where you are headed.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Platform adoption funnel: awareness through advocacy stages with health metrics

content.text: The adoption funnel tracks developer progression from awareness through advocacy. Platform API gateway logs feed daily aggregation jobs that compute MAU, team penetration, and token volume. Shadow infra rate is derived from cloud billing cross-reference. NPS pulse runs monthly via Slack. All metrics feed into a Grafana dashboard with quarterly trend lines and OKR alignment overlays.
When to use it
Quarterly business reviews: present adoption funnel and MAU trend alongside engineering leadership to justify platform investment.
Platform strategy pivots: if team penetration stagnates below 40% after 2 quarters, a strategy review is warranted — the platform may be missing capabilities needed by non-adopters.
New capability launches: measure activation funnel for new platform features specifically to understand how quickly developers adopt changes.
Shadow infra campaigns: use the shadow infra rate as a campaign metric when actively migrating teams from direct vendor API calls to the platform.
How to implement
Instrument the API gateway: every request must log developer_id, team_id, model_id, token_count, latency_ms, and timestamp — these are the raw events for all adoption metrics.
Define your denominators: document what counts as an eligible developer (anyone with a GitHub account in the AI org?) and eligible team (any team with at least one approved AI use case?).
Build the MAU and funnel dashboard in Grafana or Looker Studio from the warehouse query. Include week-over-week and quarter-over-quarter trend lines.
Implement the shadow infra rate calculation by exporting cloud billing line items tagged with LLM API calls and computing the ratio against platform-mediated spend.
Set up the monthly NPS pulse: use a Slack bot to send a 3-question survey to MAU developers, collect responses in a Google Sheet or warehouse table, and compute NPS score.
Set quarterly adoption OKRs with specific targets for MAU growth, team penetration rate, and shadow infra rate reduction.
Publish a monthly adoption summary to the engineering all-hands: 3-line summary of where adoption stands and what changed this month.
Examples

-- Monthly Active Users and team penetration for the last 6 months (BigQuery)
WITH monthly_activity AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC(request_ts, MONTH)    AS month,
    developer_id,
    team_id,
    COUNT(*)                          AS request_count,
    SUM(token_count)                  AS total_tokens
  FROM platform_api_gateway_logs
  WHERE request_ts >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 6 MONTH)
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
)
SELECT
  month,
  COUNT(DISTINCT developer_id)        AS mau,
  COUNT(DISTINCT team_id)             AS active_teams,
  SUM(total_tokens) / 1e6             AS total_tokens_millions,
  -- 90-day retention: developers active this month who were also active 3 months ago
  COUNTIF(developer_id IN (
    SELECT developer_id FROM monthly_activity
    WHERE month = DATE_SUB(month, INTERVAL 3 MONTH)
  )) * 100.0 / COUNT(DISTINCT developer_id) AS retention_90d_pct
FROM monthly_activity
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;

# Compute shadow infra rate from cloud billing export
def compute_shadow_infra_rate(
    cloud_billing_df,     # cloud provider billing with service=LLM tags
    platform_telemetry_df # platform gateway logs with token counts
) -> float:
    """
    Returns the fraction of organizational LLM spend that bypasses the platform.
    
    Shadow rate = 1 - (platform_spend / total_org_llm_spend)
    """
    # Total org LLM spend from cloud billing (includes all direct vendor calls)
    total_llm_spend = cloud_billing_df[
        cloud_billing_df["service_label"].str.contains("openai|anthropic|bedrock|vertex", case=False)
    ]["cost_usd"].sum()
    
    # Platform-mediated spend (derived from token counts and pricing)
    platform_tokens = platform_telemetry_df["token_count"].sum()
    platform_spend = platform_tokens / 1_000_000 * 3.0  # blended $/1M tokens
    
    if total_llm_spend == 0:
        return 0.0
    
    shadow_rate = 1.0 - min(platform_spend / total_llm_spend, 1.0)
    return round(shadow_rate * 100, 1)  # return as percentage
Real-world example

An e-commerce platform's AI infrastructure team had been reporting strong NPS scores (+32) for two years but was struggling to secure budget for a second engineer. When they finally measured team penetration and shadow infrastructure rate, the data told a different story: only 8 of the organization's 45 AI-eligible teams (18% penetration) were using the platform, and 65% of the organization's total LLM spend was going directly to vendors outside the platform. Their NPS was excellent among 8 teams; the other 37 teams were building without them.

They set a quarterly OKR targeting 35% team penetration and 40% shadow infra rate reduction. They assigned a DRI to run monthly adoption campaigns: identifying non-adopter teams, understanding why they had not adopted (usually missing Python RAG scaffold and no multi-modal support), and fixing the top blockers. Within two quarters, penetration reached 31% and shadow infra dropped to 50%. This data secured two additional engineer headcounts in the annual planning cycle.
Common mistakes
Reporting MAU without denominator context: MAU of 50 looks very different if your eligible developer pool is 60 versus 500.
Conflating engagement with adoption: a single team making millions of API calls inflates MAU without reflecting organizational spread.
Ignoring the shadow infrastructure rate: teams that are not using the platform but are doing LLM work represent the highest-priority adoption opportunity and the highest organizational risk.
Not tracking churn: developers who were active 90 days ago and are no longer active represent regressions in platform quality or capability — they should be surveyed immediately.
Optimizing activation without ensuring retention: aggressively onboarding teams that churn within 60 days inflates short-term numbers while masking a quality problem.
Best practices
Track both MAU and team penetration rate — absolute user count and organizational breadth are both necessary to understand adoption health.
Measure shadow infra rate quarterly by cross-referencing cloud billing with platform telemetry — this is the most honest measure of platform relevance.
Run a monthly churn investigation: any developer who was in the last 3 months' MAU but is absent this month gets a brief outreach to understand why.
Set adoption OKRs with equal weight to new activation and 90-day retention — both must grow for the platform to compound value.
Publish the adoption dashboard broadly within engineering, not just to the platform team — visibility creates organizational pull toward the platform.
Performance and scalability

Adoption metric computation is a standard analytics workload: daily batch jobs over gateway logs, monthly NPS aggregation, and quarterly billing cross-reference. These scale linearly with usage volume and can run in under 5 minutes even for organizations with hundreds of developers and millions of API calls per month. The bottleneck is not computation but data completeness — missing team_id tags in gateway logs will cause penetration rate undercounting.

As the platform scales past 100 active developers, consider implementing developer cohort analysis (grouping developers by onboarding week) to detect whether the newest cohorts are activating and retaining at the same rate as earlier cohorts. Declining activation or retention in recent cohorts is an early warning of capability gaps or quality regressions affecting new use cases that early adopters never encountered.

Segment adoption metrics by use-case archetype (agent apps, RAG apps, fine-tuned models, embeddings) in addition to team. Teams doing agentic work often have very different activation and retention patterns from teams doing simple embedding search. Reporting blended adoption numbers can hide that the platform is excelling at one archetype while failing at another. Per-archetype breakdowns guide which scaffold types and capabilities to prioritize for the next quarter.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
API Gateway logs
Primary adoption data source
Must log developer_id, team_id per request
BigQuery / Redshift
Metric computation
Daily scheduled queries for MAU, funnel, retention
Grafana / Looker Studio
Adoption dashboard
Embed quarterly OKR targets as threshold lines
Slack bot (custom)
Monthly NPS pulse delivery
Target developers with >= 1 request in last 30 days
Cloud Billing Export
Shadow infra rate computation
GCP/AWS billing tagged by service for LLM calls
Linear / Jira
Churn and non-adopter investigation
Create investigation tickets for churned developers
Comparison with alternatives
Metric approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Adoption funnel with shadow infra rate
Full picture: usage, spread, and shadow
Shadow infra rate requires billing access
MAU only
Simple, always available
Hides adoption breadth; inflated by power users
NPS only
Direct satisfaction signal
No coverage or shadow infra information
Ticket volume proxy
No instrumentation needed
Undercounts silent usage; biased toward troublesome features
Exec-reported team count
Easy to communicate
Anecdotal; no funnel or retention visibility
Security and reliability

Gateway logs used for adoption metrics contain metadata about what developers are building with the platform. Access should be limited to the platform team and engineering leadership. Strip prompt content and model output from adoption analytics tables — these tables should contain counts, tokens, and metadata only, never actual LLM conversation content.

For reliability: if gateway logging fails for even a few hours, MAU for that period will be undercounted, affecting trend data. Set up a logging health check that alerts if log volume drops more than 20% below the prior-week average, and handle gaps in trend charts explicitly rather than interpolating.
Key takeaways
Platform adoption metrics track the developer funnel from awareness through advocacy, with MAU, team penetration, NPS, and shadow infra rate as the core measures.
Shadow infrastructure rate is the most honest measure of platform relevance — it reveals how much LLM work is happening outside the platform's visibility.
Team penetration rate requires a denominator: the count of AI-eligible teams, which must be tracked separately and updated quarterly.
90-day retention is the most important growth metric — it distinguishes compound platform adoption from churn-heavy shallow activation.
Publish adoption metrics broadly within engineering, not just to the platform team — organizational visibility creates pull toward platform adoption.
Set quarterly OKRs for both new activation and 90-day retention; optimizing only activation without retention does not produce durable growth.
Investigate churned developers monthly: a developer who was active but stopped using the platform is the highest-signal feedback source available.
